article.title: OpenAI Launches ChatGPT Personal Finance Preview With a Plaid-Powered Dashboard for US Pro Users
article.summary: OpenAI began rolling out a personal finance experience inside ChatGPT on May 15, letting US Pro subscribers connect over 12,000 institutions through Plaid and see a unified dashboard of spending, subscriptions, portfolio and upcoming payments.
article.body_markdown:
## Overview

OpenAI on May 15 began rolling out a personal finance experience inside ChatGPT that lets users link their real bank, brokerage and credit card accounts and ask the assistant questions grounded in their actual money, according to [TechCrunch](https://techcrunch.com/2026/05/15/openai-launches-chatgpt-for-personal-finance-will-let-you-connect-bank-accounts/). The preview is limited to ChatGPT Pro subscribers in the United States and is available on the web and on iOS, per [MacRumors](https://www.macrumors.com/2026/05/15/chatgpt-personal-finance/). It is OpenAI's first attempt to plug ChatGPT directly into a consumer's financial accounts, and follows the company's April acqui-hire of personal-finance startup Hiro, as [previously reported](/article/2026-04/15-openai-acquires-ai-personal-finance-startup-hiro-in-acqui-hire-as-chatgpt-pushes-deeper-into-consumer-money-management).

## What we know

Account connections are handled by Plaid, the data-aggregation service that already powers fintech apps such as Venmo, Robinhood, Chime, Coinbase, Betterment and SoFi. OpenAI says the integration covers more than 12,000 financial institutions, including Schwab, Fidelity, Chase, Robinhood, American Express and Capital One, per [TechCrunch](https://techcrunch.com/2026/05/15/openai-launches-chatgpt-for-personal-finance-will-let-you-connect-bank-accounts/). Plaid said the experience supports checking, savings, crypto wallets and investment accounts on its [own corporate blog](https://plaid.com/blog/chatgpt-personal-finance-plaid/) about the launch.

Once accounts are linked, ChatGPT exposes a dedicated "Finances" surface with a dashboard covering portfolio performance, spending, subscriptions and upcoming payments, according to [TechCrunch](https://techcrunch.com/2026/05/15/openai-launches-chatgpt-for-personal-finance-will-let-you-connect-bank-accounts/). Users can either select "Get started" inside the Finances sidebar option or type "@Finances, connect my accounts" directly into a conversation. [MacRumors](https://www.macrumors.com/2026/05/15/chatgpt-personal-finance/) lists sample prompts the company is highlighting, including "Help me build a plan to buy a house in my area in the next 5 years" and "What did my recent vacation actually cost me?"

Access is read-only on the financial side. ChatGPT can see balances, transactions, investments and liabilities, but as [Engadget](https://www.engadget.com/2173768/chatgpt-will-offer-personalized-financial-advice-if-you-connect-your-bank-account/) notes, it "can't see full account numbers or make changes to accounts." The same outlet quotes OpenAI's pitch for why it built the feature: "every month more than 200 million users already turn to ChatGPT for guidance on managing their money," a figure Plaid also cites on [its blog](https://plaid.com/blog/chatgpt-personal-finance-plaid/).

The feature runs on GPT-5.5, OpenAI's reasoning-tuned successor flagged in the announcement, per [SiliconANGLE](https://siliconangle.com/2026/05/15/openai-previews-personal-finance-features-chatgpt-pro/) and [The Tech Portal](https://thetechportal.com/2026/05/15/openai-rolls-out-chatgpt-personal-finance-tool-with-real-time-bank-account-connectivity/). OpenAI says it built a custom benchmark with finance professionals to tune the model for money questions, although the company did not publish a public score that matches across press accounts. Intuit support is on the roadmap, [TechCrunch](https://techcrunch.com/2026/05/15/openai-launches-chatgpt-for-personal-finance-will-let-you-connect-bank-accounts/) reports, which OpenAI says will eventually let the assistant analyze tax impacts and credit card approval odds.

On data handling, ChatGPT keeps each user's financial context in a discrete "Finances" memory that users can view and delete. Disconnecting an account removes the synced data within 30 days, [TechCrunch](https://techcrunch.com/2026/05/15/openai-launches-chatgpt-for-personal-finance-will-let-you-connect-bank-accounts/) reports, and temporary chats do not draw on the financial memory at all, per [Engadget](https://www.engadget.com/2173768/chatgpt-will-offer-personalized-financial-advice-if-you-connect-your-bank-account/).

## What OpenAI and Plaid are saying

In a joint write-up on [Plaid's blog](https://plaid.com/blog/chatgpt-personal-finance-plaid/), Ty Geri, a product lead at OpenAI, framed the integration in terms of personalization: "Finances are deeply personal and shaped by individual goals, priorities, and everyday realities." Plaid CTO Will Robinson described the technical preconditions in the same post: "Bringing it to life requires three things working together: broad financial data coverage, the ability to make sense of that data, and consumer trust."

OpenAI is positioning the rollout as a limited test rather than a finished product. [Engadget](https://www.engadget.com/2173768/chatgpt-will-offer-personalized-financial-advice-if-you-connect-your-bank-account/) reports the company plans to extend access to Plus subscribers after gathering feedback from Pro users. According to [TestingCatalog](https://www.testingcatalog.com/openai-launches-personal-finance-on-chatgpt-for-pro-users/), OpenAI is explicit that the assistant is not meant to substitute for a human professional: "This feature is not intended to replace a financial adviser."

## What we don't know

Several details remain unclear. Press accounts disagree on benchmark numbers — [SiliconANGLE](https://siliconangle.com/2026/05/15/openai-previews-personal-finance-features-chatgpt-pro/) cites a 60 percent score on an external FinanceAgent benchmark while [TestingCatalog](https://www.testingcatalog.com/openai-launches-personal-finance-on-chatgpt-for-pro-users/) cites internal scores of 79 and 82.5 out of 100 for GPT-5.5 Thinking and GPT-5.5 Pro respectively — and OpenAI's official methodology has not been independently verified. The company has not disclosed a timeline for the broader rollout to Plus subscribers, when Intuit support will arrive, or whether the feature will remain US-only in the long term. Disclosures about how Plaid-mediated transaction data interacts with OpenAI's broader model-training pipeline have also not been spelled out in detail in the materials made public on launch day.

## Analysis

The preview turns a usage pattern OpenAI has already acknowledged — hundreds of millions of monthly questions about money — into a product surface with first-party data behind it. The bet is straightforward: with read access to a user's actual cash flow, portfolio, subscriptions and upcoming bills, a reasoning model can move from generic advice ("build an emergency fund") to specific guidance ("you have a $46 streaming subscription you haven't used in three months"). It is also a direct extension of the Hiro acqui-hire reported in April, which brought Digit founder Ethan Bloch's team in-house just weeks before this launch.

The positioning against incumbents is sharper than the announcement suggests. Plaid's own framing notes that "more than half of Americans say managing money without AI will soon feel outdated," per [its blog post](https://plaid.com/blog/chatgpt-personal-finance-plaid/), and the company has spent a decade wiring bank APIs into apps such as Venmo and Robinhood. Plugging that pipe directly into ChatGPT effectively turns a general-purpose assistant into a competitor to budgeting tools, robo-advisors and the personalization layers that banks themselves have been building. Whether that competition is welcomed by the named institutions — several of which run their own AI assistants — will likely shape how the integration evolves beyond the Pro tier.
